dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, en route for a Breathing Problem at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I started having shortness of breath while I was asleep about 20 minutes ago.
It came on suddenly again. I've been coughing but haven't brought up any sputum or discharge.
Can you tell me your name and the year right now?
Yes, I'm Pat. It's 1964.
Show me how you can move your fingers.
Here we go. I can move and feel both hands and feet.
You're alert, can speak clearly, and follow commands. AOX4, GCS 15.
When you woke up with shortness of breath, was there chest pain, dizziness, or fever?
I just felt SOB all the sudden, like the other week when I was admitted to [COMPANY_NAME] for CHF.
Can we go over your medical history a bit?
I have COPD, diabetes, lupus, anemia, hypertension, anxiety, and depression. I haven't been intubated.
What medications are you prescribed now?
I take Lipitor, Neurontin, Plaquenil, Prozac, Lotrel, Catapres, and two types of insulin.
You're not allergic to anything, right?
I'm allergic to aspirin.
Your breathing looks labored. It's 26 breaths per minute right now.
Heart rate is elevated at 106 beats per minute.
Blood pressure initially read 0/0 but rose to 194 over 97.
Oxygen saturation is low at 80% on room air.
Glucose check shows high levels at 573 mg/dL.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia without signs of ACS.
Your airway is clear and patent with no signs of obstruction.
Breathing is shallow, rapid, and you're using accessory muscles to breathe.
There is audible wheezing heard bilaterally, more at the bases.
Oxygen is being administered via NRB now to help improve your saturation.
Radial pulse is strong and regular. Skin is warm and dry with good perfusion.
A 4-lead ECG placed to monitor your heart rhythm, as ordered by protocol.
You're now on an NRB mask at 15LPM. Oxygen levels will continue to be monitored.
I see the NRB mask is working. Your SpO2 appears to be improving some on the monitor.
I'll help apply a nebulizer with Albuterol and Atrovent to help open your airways.
That should help the wheezing and make breathing easier moving forward.
Do you have any issues with urination or genital symptoms?
No, urination is normal and I haven't seen any blood.
May I listen to your chest again while you breathe slowly?
Alright, just do it. Breathing is still very hard.
I don't see any rashes or bruises. Your skin color and turgor are normal.
BP is 182/104 now with stable SpO2, 100% on NRB.
Let's attempt to start an IV in your left arm for possible fluid management.
The IV flashed but wasn't advanced properly, so we've moved here.
Next, I'll try placing an IV on your right arm for easier access.
The second IV attempt also didn't go in, but we'll keep trying if needed later.
A 12-lead EKG was performed. It showed sinus tach with some LVH findings but no STEMI.
SpO2 remains at 100%, BP is elevated, and you have no chest pain.
You haven't reported any new pain. How are you feeling compared to before?
I'm less dizzy now and oxygen seems better, but I'm still very tired and short of breath.
The nebulizer is still running and your vitals are tracking through the monitor.
Where would you like to go for care, Ms. Pat?
I want to go to [COMPANY_NAME]. I just left there.
We'll now move you to the stretcher and secure for transport. It might be a bit cold.
You'll be immobilized using 5-point restraints and placed in an upright position.
You'll be moved to the ambulance via the steps with your feet first and secured into the cot.
We are transporting a 55-year-old female to Trauma Bay 8 at [COMPANY_NAME] for breathing issues.
She has a history of COPD, lupus, CHF, and diabetes. Oxygen improved her saturation to 100%.
Nebulizer was given, IVs unsuccessful, and 12-lead showed sinus tach with normal findings. No pain reported.
Blood sugar was 573 at first but we didn't give fluid due to concerns for fluid overload.
Continued chief complaint is shortness of breath with no reported pain.
Vitals remained stable with repeat checks: heart rate around 104 and BP still elevated.
She's secured and we're ready for transport. Making one final check of gear and records.
